title: Quiz: scale up vs scale out, and when to shard
prompt_md: What's the difference between scaling up and scaling out, and when do you reach for sharding?
options: Scale up and scale out are the same thing; sharding is just another word for caching
Scale out means turning servers off to save cost; you shard to reduce the number of databases
Scale up means adding more servers; scale out means buying a bigger CPU; you shard whenever traffic increases at all
Scale up = a bigger single machine (simple but capped); scale out = more machines behind a load balancer; you shard/partition the data only when it's too big or write-heavy for a single node
explanation_md: **Scaling up (vertical)** means giving one machine more CPU/RAM — simple and needs no code changes, but it hits a **ceiling** and stays a single point of failure. **Scaling out (horizontal)** means adding more machines behind a **load balancer**, which requires the app to be **stateless** so any node can serve any request. **Sharding (partitioning)** splits the *data* across nodes by a **shard key** and is the last resort — you reach for it only when the dataset or write volume genuinely exceeds what one node can hold, because cross-shard queries get complex. The interview-safe ordering: cache and read replicas, then scale out the app, then shard.
